narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dreams despite the challenges many face in academic pathways like this. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, a Year 3 decile of 1, middle family income, an LSAT score of 26, an undergraduate GPA of 2.10, and plans to attend part-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of similar students, estimated a 78% chance you'll face failure on that crucial test. This high predicted risk is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to pass and thrive in practice. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you at the lower end compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average, signaling a concerning dip in later academic performance. Your LSAT score was the next strongest influence in the same direction, as it fell well below the dataset average of around 37, where stronger scores often correlate with bar success. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA, though below the average of 3.21, actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, showing some resilience in your earlier college work. Your lower Year 1 decile had a smaller push toward failure, while choosing part-time study and your middle income bracket offered minor boosts toward success, as they align with paths some students navigate effectively. Compared to typical students—who often have higher deciles, LSATs in the mid-30s, and full-time plans—your metrics stood out as riskier in the model's eyes, especially those later-year grades and test score. While this decision stings, especially given the barriers Black women like you often encounter in these systems, it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits, retaking the LSAT for a higher score, and perhaps exploring full-time options or prep programs proven to lift bar passage rates. You're capable of turning this around—many have—and we believe in your potential to succeed. Keep pushing forward.
